one.zh: PRACTICE & PROCEDURE – Transcripts – where mother makes an oral application for provision of transcripts –where parties and their legal representative…
one.en: PRACTICE & PROCEDURE – Transcripts – where mother makes an oral application for provision of transcripts –where parties and their legal representative…

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE & PROCEDURE – Transcripts – where mother makes an oral application for provision of transcripts –where parties and their legal representatives permitted to view those transcripts already held by the Court in the Subpoena Registry – where application is otherwise dismissed. FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE & PROCEDURE – s102NA – where final hearing is part heard following 17 days of hearing with a further 5 days estimated – where father’s s102NA practitioners filed a Notice of Ceasing to Act without seeking leave to do so – where father has successfully found new legal representation under the s102NA Scheme – where those solicitors have filed a Notice of Address for Service – where father intends to continue to retain the same barrister for trial – leave granted. FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE & PROCEDURE – s102NA – where mother’s s102NA practitioners also filed a Notice of Ceasing to Act without seeking leave to do so – where mother says relationship has broken down – where mother does not intend to retain the same Counsel – practitioners granted leave to withdraw – where mother tells the Court she has engaged with new solicitors and Senior Counsel – where those practitioners have not filed a Notice of Address for Service – where the s102NA Scheme at Victoria Legal Aid do not confirm the mother’s new legal representation as described by her– where mother advised of the risks of continuing without legal representation including that she cannot personally cross-examine the father.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

Ex-tempore

BENNETT J

This matter comes before me for a Case Management Hearing in the midst of a part-heard final hearing as to parenting arrangements for Y (aged 10 years) and X (aged 12 years). I have heard 17 days of evidence over the period 15 September 2025 to 11 December 2025. The matter is set down to continue for a further 5 days commencing 25 May 2026.
During the first 17 days of the trial, the mother was represented by Ms Natalie Vogel of Counsel, instructed by Cohen Lawyers with funding pursuant to the s102NA Scheme. The father was represented by Mr Howe of Counsel instructed by TFA Legal. The Independent Children’s Lawyer throughout these proceedings (including the previous parenting proceedings) has been Ms Hams of Southern Family Law. Ms Mansfield of Counsel appears for the Independent Children’s Lawyer.

The father’s legal representation

On 13 February 2026, TFA Legal filed a Notice of Ceasing to Act. No leave was sought for TFA Legal to withdraw. Rule 3.10 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 provides that:
(1A) A lawyer must not file a Notice under subrule (1) within 14 days before the day fixed for a final hearing except with the leave of the court, which must be sought in writing.
It is uncontroversial that leave is required in these circumstances. The final hearing is well-advanced. Leave was not sought and my Chambers was not notified of the filing of the Notice of Ceasing to Act.
Correspondence received by my Chambers from TFA Legal includes an email from the father to the Cross-Examination Scheme in which he stated that the solicitor responsible for the matter has ceased employment with TFA Lawyers and there is no other solicitor at that firm able to take on the matter and that BTT Lawyers was taking over the matter. I mark that email Exhibit C3. This is the father’s third set of lawyers under the Scheme however there is no indication from TFA Lawyers that the transfer was due to any breakdown in the relationship between the father and his legal team.
Conveniently and sensibly, the father and BTT Lawyers intend to continue to retain Mr Howe for the remainder of the hearing. The difficulty for the father’s new solicitors is that the father is presently under cross-examination and, therefore, cannot provide them with instructions. This is principally mediated through the continued retention of the father’s Counsel who has been present for the 17 days of final hearing.

The mother’s legal representation

On 5 March 2026, Cohen Lawyers, the solicitor for the mother, likewise filed a Notice of Ceasing to Act. Again, no leave was sought for the solicitors to withdraw. Chambers was notified of the withdrawal by email from the mother on 16 March 2026 in which she sought production of transcripts. In that email, the mother stated:
I do not have an instructing solicitor present at Court and my legal representation has now ceased. I am currently self-represented while in the process of securing replacement representation under my existing section 102NA funding.
Previously, the mother has engaged Lander & Rogers, Testart Family Lawyers, SLF Lawyers and Wallamatta Legal to act on her behalf in these proceedings commenced 2023. Under the s102NA scheme, the mother has previously been represented by Bramham Lawyers (until 29 August 2025) and then by Cohen Lawyers. I understand that Chambers has been advised by the s102NA Scheme that the mother will be granted one further transfer and that any further request to transfer will be rejected.
I gather that the mother has had some difficulty with representation.
At a Case Management Hearing on 20 November 2025, the mother’s solicitors, through the mother’s Counsel, sought a restraint on the mother from emailing the instructor. The matter had been adjourned part-heard whilst the mother was being cross-examined. Ms Vogel informed the Court that the mother was attempting to obtain legal advice from her solicitor while under cross-examination and that the solicitor was concerned that it could put her practicing certificate “at risk”. The submission was framed as an application, but the mother was not given notice of the application, and it was made in the absence of the mother in Court. Upon the mother re-joining the Court link, the mother stated she was ‘mortified’. I refused to grant leave to the mother’s solicitors to make an oral application for an injunction and advised the mother that she should not contact her solicitor and seek advice in such a way that compromises’ the solicitor’s duty as an officer of the court to abide by the rule that there is to be no discussions with a client under cross-examination as to the substance of the client’s evidence.
The representative from Cohen Lawyers today confirmed that there has been a breakdown in the solicitor-client relationship. She advised that she received instructions from the mother upon which she was unable to act and Victoria Legal Aid refused further funding. She further advised that the mother instructed her to file a Notice of Ceasing to Act and that she has provided the mother with access to her file, other than the father’s bank statements which can only be released to the mother’s solicitors.
The mother today informed the Court that she does not intend to retain Ms Vogel as Counsel and that the relationship has broken down irretrievably. Ms Vogel submits that she is willing to continue to appear in the proceeding.
The mother states that her new solicitors are Tyler, Tipping and Woods (“TT Woods”) of Gippsland, Victoria and that she has briefed Ms Mooney of his Majesty’s Counsel.
Enquiries made by my Chambers to Victoria Legal Aid during this hearing indicate that the mother’s file is instead currently being offered to Yarra Family Lawyers. TT Woods are on the panel of solicitors eligible to accept s102NA matters but do not hold the file. The mother indicated that she would like to verify this information and sought to contact Victoria Legal Aid via email during the hearing to do so. In any event, no solicitor has yet been formally engaged by the mother and no Notice of Address for Service has been filed on behalf of the mother.
No one opposed the practitioners obtaining leave. I have given both parents’ solicitors leave to withdraw.
Orders of Chief Justice Alstergren made on 4 February 2025 provide that pursuant to s102NA(1)(c)(iv) of the Family Law Act, the Applicant is not permitted to personally cross-examine the Respondent and the Respondent is not permitted to personally cross-examine the Applicant. The cross-examination of the father is due to recommence on 25 May 2026. The mother will not be permitted to continue the cross-examination of the father without a lawyer. Therefore, if the mother fails to retain legal representation under the s102NA Cross-Examination Scheme through Victoria Legal Aid, and she intends to cross-examine the father, the mother will need to engage the services of a lawyer who may need to be funded privately from her own resources. Foregoing cross-examination of the opposing party is a very serious step and a very serious curtailment of her entitlement to test the father’s case, particularly as I infer that the mother was not satisfied with Ms Vogel’s cross-examination to date.
The mother’s previous solicitors notified the mother of their intention to withdraw in early March 2026. The mother today consents to their application for leave to withdraw and states she has no intention of continuing to brief Ms Vogel of Counsel. The mother will have approximately 6 weeks to find, secure and instruct new practitioners. She is not a stranger to the Cross-Examination Scheme and has been in correspondence with the administrators of that Scheme. I take this opportunity to remind the mother that refusing her the opportunity to continue cross‑examination of the father and to test his case may place her at a significant strategic disadvantage.

The mother’s application for copies of transcripts of final hearing

The mother emailed my Chambers on 16 March 2026 advising that she was self-represented and requesting a copy of the Court transcript for the previous 17 days of hearing.
The mother’s email is extracted below:
I write to respectfully request that the transcripts of the trial proceedings to date be provided to me.

As the Court will be aware, transcripts were previously provided to Mr Howe, Counsel for the Father, upon request. I understand that request was made on the basis that he did not have an instructing solicitor present at Court and due to the length of time that had elapsed between hearing dates. Those circumstances apply in my case, and noting an increased time elapsed since our last hearing date in December 2025.

I do not have an instructing solicitor present at Court and my legal representation has now ceased. I am currently self-represented while in the process of securing replacement representation under my existing section 102NA funding.

A significant amount of time has now elapsed since the last hearing dates in December 2025, together with the adjournments that occurred in January and March this year. Access to the transcript is therefore necessary to ensure accuracy of the record and to enable proper preparation for the continuation of the hearing. Given the matter is presently listed to resume in May, provision of the transcript will assist incoming counsel to prepare, including for the purposes of cross-examination.

I understand that provision of the transcript will also assist the efficient continuation of the trial and avoid unnecessary time being spent revisiting evidence when the matter resumes.

Given that transcripts were previously provided to Counsel for the Father upon request, I respectfully seek the same accommodation to ensure procedural fairness between the parties.

I also note that, as Your Honour is aware, I do not have the financial capacity to privately purchase the transcripts.

In these circumstances, I respectfully request that the transcripts of the trial proceedings to date be provided.
This is not the first time the mother has requested copies of transcripts. On 14 January 2026, the mother’s Counsel emailed Chambers with the following request:
Also, may we please have all transcripts (that have not yet been provided) for the hearing to date emailed to us or placed on the e-brief, as soon as practicable? My client is legally aided, so I request that they be provided without fee.
The mother’s case ran from 15 September until 10 December 2025 (16 days of the 17 days heard thus far).
The Court holds transcripts for the following days of hearing:
(1) 15 September 2025;

(2) 16 September 2025;

(3) 17 September 2025;

(4) 18 September 2025;

(5) 19 September 2025;

(6) 22 September 2025;

(7) 23 September 2025;

(8) 24 September 2025;

(9) 27 November 2025

(10) 28 November 2025.

The transcript of 16 September 2025 was provided to the legal practitioners on 15 December 2025 in the context of an oral application made by Mr Howe of Counsel. The transcript of 18 September 2025 was provided to the legal practitioners pursuant to an Order in the circumstances where the mother was unwell and unable to continue the hearing on that day. The proceedings that day consisted only of housekeeping. The remainder of the above listed days of transcript have not been previously made accessible to the parties.
The Court does not currently hold transcripts for the following days of hearing:
(1) 23 September 2025;

(2) 25 September 2025;

(3) 4 December 2025

(4) 5 December 2025

(5) 8 December 2205

(6) 9 December 2025;

(7) 10 December 2025;

(8) 11 December 2025.

The Court policy on provision of transcript is as follows:[1]
The Court does not provide transcripts to litigants at the Court's expense.

In limited circumstances, if the Court has already obtained a transcript, with the permission of the Court parties and/or their legal representatives may be permitted to peruse (but not copy) the Court's transcript in the court registry. The Court does not order transcript for all events so access to the transcript on the Court file may not always be available.

Occasionally the transcript of proceedings will constitute the reasons. In such instances, if reasons are required for the purposes of an appeal, the Court may provide a copy of the transcript, or portions of the transcript, to the parties and the Appeal Court for the purpose of the appeal.
Even in an appeal context, where it is a requirement that the applicant file and serve a copy of the transcript pursuant to r 13.19, the Court will not provide transcripts at its own cost in “anything other than exceptional cases”.[2]
I understand that transcript for one day of trial, at the slowest turnaround of 10 days costs approximately $2,000 a per day of hearing. However, there is some doubt as to whether the transcripts would or could be prepared. The providers of transcript known to the parties, VIQ Solutions, is in voluntary administration. I understand that they are not working to their previous capacity and I am unsure when orders for transcripts would be filled.
The nine days of trial for which the Court holds transcript can be made available for inspection by the parties in the subpoena document inspection area of Registry. The parties and/or their practitioners will not be permitted to take copies of the transcripts but they can read them.
The Court does not hold transcripts for the balance of the proceeding. The Court does not have the funds available to it to order transcript for parties in all litigation that comes before it. In my view, this matter does not qualify under any exceptional circumstance for requiring transcript where the party concerned has been able to consider the implications of withdrawing instructions from their solicitors who have previously acted and Counsel who have previously appeared.
I note that authorised audio recordings of matters heard in Court are available in exceptional circumstances when approved by the relevant Registry Manager. Likewise, no copies can be made of the audio recordings.

Information Sharing

Counsel for the Independent Children’s Lawyer informed the Court that there is another investigation being undertaken by the Department of Families, Fairness and Housing in relation to the children. The Independent Children’s Lawyer seeks an Order under s67ZBE. This was not opposed. I will make the Order.
